senses.definition: plural of prolations: acts of propping up or setting forth; in rhetoric or philosophy, anticipatory or forward-looking statements or projections
examples: The historian studied the prolations of the theory to future events.
The prolation of the plan included timelines and projections.

senses.definition: relating to or indicating forward movement or forward-looking orientation; used in rhetoric to describe forward arguments or claims
examples: A prolative approach anticipates future developments.
Her prolative reasoning set the stage for upcoming policy changes.

senses.definition: in a forward-looking or anticipatory manner
examples: The project was prolatively planned to avoid delays.
She spoke prolatively about future innovations.

senses.definition: a member of the working class; often used in Marxist contexts to refer to the laboring class
examples: The factory workers were members of the prole class.
In the novel, the protagonist joins the prole community.

senses.definition: a name used for a professional league in sports or gaming; context-specific and uncommon outside particular communities
examples: The team joined the proleague this season.
She followed several matches on the proleague schedule.

senses.definition: a person who advocates for or supports the prole class; a familiar or informal term for a working-class ally
examples: He described himself as a proleaguer in discussions about labor rights.
The speaker urged proleaguer voices to be heard.

senses.definition: a rarely used or specialized term; may refer to a mineral or geological specimen in some contexts
examples: The geologist identified a rare prolectite specimen.

senses.definition: having prolepses or foreknowledge; advanced warning or anticipation applied to a person or plan
examples: The proled plan saved time by anticipating obstacles.

senses.definition: a preliminary or introductory section, often used in academic works to introduce concepts before the main text
examples: The proleg laid out the scope of the study.
In the proleg, the author explains key terms.

senses.definition: to appoint as a proxy or to delegate forward, often in formal or ceremonial contexts
examples: The council prolegates the duties to the deputy.
They prolegate responsibilities during the transition.

senses.definition: relating to the process or act of proposing legislation; preceding or introductory to legislation
examples: A prolegislative debate shaped the bill.
The committee issued a prolegislative report.

senses.definition: an introduction or preface to a book, especially a scholarly one; essential background information
examples: The prolegomena outlined the main arguments.
Scholars often read the prolegomena before the chapters.

senses.definition: relating to prolegomena; introductory or foundational in nature
examples: The prolegomenal discussion clarified the framework.

senses.definition: pertaining to prolegomena; introductory in nature
examples: The prolegomenary chapter sets the stage.

senses.definition: a person who writes or studies prolegomena; a scholar focusing on introductory matters
examples: The professor is a noted prolegomenist in philosophy.

senses.definition: a preliminary statement or introduction in a scholarly work; an opening set of remarks
examples: The prolegomenon explains the book's aims.
Readers should start with the prolegomenon to understand the framework.

senses.definition: plural or a fossilized form of prolegomenon usage; may appear as a rare or experimental term in some texts
examples: Some editors noted prolegomenona in the manuscript.

senses.definition: relating to introductory matters or prolegomena; introductory in character
examples: The prolegomenous sections frame the main argument.

senses.definition: the paired stubby legs of caterpillars and some insect larvae
examples: The caterpillar used its prolegs to grip the leaf.

senses.definition: a made-up or rare term; likely meant to reference leniency or prolepsis-related concepts; not standard in English
examples: The text hints at proleniency but uses an unclear term.

senses.definition: plural of prolepsis; in narrative theory, mental anticipations or foreshadowings of future events
examples: The novel uses prolepses to hint at the twist.
Foreshadowing through prolepses creates suspense.

senses.definition: a figure of speech in which a future event is anticipated or a future action is described as if it has already occurred
examples: The prolepsis of the hero’s victory startled the audience.
In rhetoric, prolepsis can argue a point by anticipating objections.

senses.definition: relating to or denoting a person or thing that anticipates future events; projecting forward in time
examples: A proleptic interpretation looks ahead to later developments.

senses.definition: of or relating to prolepsis; anticipatory in nature
examples: The proleptical argument foresees objections.

senses.definition: in an anticipatory or foretelling manner
examples: The author spoke proleptically, hinting at later events.
